dimensions.title: Emotional Intelligence
dimensions.description: Measures your ability to recognize, understand, and manage emotions in yourself and in your relationships. High EQ predicts better conflict resolution, deeper emotional attunement, and more satisfying partnerships.
dimensions.scientific_basis: Adapted from the Wong & Law Emotional Intelligence Scale (WLEIS; Wong & Law, 2002), a 16-item validated measure of EQ widely used in organizational and relationship research. Items reframed from workplace to romantic relationship contexts. Supplemented by findings from Brackett, Warner, & Bosco (2005) linking EQ to relationship satisfaction.
dimensions.compatibility_type_rationale: Both partners benefit from similar EQ levels, but low-low pairings are high risk. A minimum floor of 2.5 (on 5-point scale) is enforced: if either partner scores below the floor, a penalty is applied. High-high pairings score highest; mismatched pairings where one partner significantly outscores the other create frustration for the higher-EQ partner and shame for the lower.

sub_scales.title: Self-Awareness
sub_scales.description: Recognizing your own emotions and understanding how they influence your behavior in relationships.

sub_scales.title: Empathy
sub_scales.description: Reading and responding to your partner's emotional state, even when they haven't spelled it out.

sub_scales.title: Emotional Regulation
sub_scales.description: Managing your emotional reactions under stress, especially during conflict or disappointment.

questions.text: I can usually tell the difference between being angry at my partner and being angry about something else that's spilling over into the relationship.
When I'm being distant or short with someone I'm dating, I usually know why.
I sometimes catch myself being moody and have no idea where it's coming from.
I know which situations tend to trigger my worst reactions in a relationship (jealousy, insecurity, shutting down).
After an argument, I can honestly assess what part of the problem was mine.
I can usually sense when my partner is upset even before they say anything.
When my partner is going through something difficult, I find it easy to put myself in their shoes.
Honestly, I sometimes struggle to understand why my partner is upset about something that seems minor to me.
When someone I'm dating shares something vulnerable, my instinct is to listen and validate rather than jump to fixing it.
I pay attention to the little changes in my partner's tone or energy, not just their words.
When I'm upset about something my partner did, I can usually pause before reacting.
In the heat of an argument, I tend to say things I later regret.
When a conversation starts getting heated, I can bring down the temperature without shutting down or walking away.
If I'm having a bad day, I'm pretty good at not taking it out on the person I'm with.
When I feel myself getting jealous or insecure, I can sit with that feeling instead of immediately acting on it.

scoring.sub_scale_computation: Each sub-scale score = mean of its 5 items (after reversals). Range: 1.0 - 5.0.
scoring.dimension_score_computation: Dimension score = mean of all 15 items (after reversals). Range: 1.0 - 5.0.
scoring.compatibility_function: similarity_with_floor: (1) If either partner scores below floor_threshold (2.5), apply floor_penalty multiplier (0.6) to the pair score. (2) Compute raw similarity = 1 - (|score_A - score_B| / 4). (3) Apply level bonus: avg_score_normalized = (avg(score_A, score_B) - 1) / 4; pair_score = raw_similarity * (0.7 + 0.3 * avg_score_normalized). (4) If floor triggered, final_score = pair_score * floor_penalty. This rewards high-high pairings, penalizes low-low pairings, and moderately scores mismatched pairs.

dimensions.title: Lifestyle & Ambition
dimensions.description: Captures how you prefer to spend your time, energy, and attention. Mismatches here don't cause dramatic fights -- they cause slow erosion as one partner feels dragged along or held back.
dimensions.scientific_basis: Draws from Big Five personality subscales: Conscientiousness (industriousness, orderliness) and Openness to Experience (aesthetic sensitivity, intellectual curiosity, adventurousness), adapted from the IPIP-NEO-120 (Johnson, 2014). Social energy items adapted from the Eysenck Personality Questionnaire Extraversion subscale. Framing shifted from trait measurement to lifestyle preference expression for relationship context.
dimensions.compatibility_type_rationale: Lifestyle compatibility is about shared rhythm. Two homebodies thrive together; two adventurers thrive together. But one of each creates daily friction over how to spend weekends, vacations, and free time. Unlike values or attachment, there is no complementarity benefit -- you are simply more compatible with someone who wants a similar life.

sub_scales.title: Pace of Life
sub_scales.description: How driven and fast-paced you are versus how much you prioritize presence and ease.

sub_scales.title: Social Energy
sub_scales.description: How much social interaction you want as a couple and how you recharge.

sub_scales.title: Future Orientation
sub_scales.description: How much you plan ahead versus go with the flow, especially around career and life goals.

questions.text: A perfect Saturday is packed with activities, not open-ended.
I feel restless if I go a full weekend without being productive.
My ideal vacation involves doing as little as possible.
I'd rather be overcommitted than bored.
I regularly take on new projects, side hustles, or goals outside of my main job.
I love hosting friends at our place or going out as a couple most weekends.
After a long work week, I'd rather stay in with my partner than meet up with friends.
I need a good amount of solo time to feel like myself, even when I'm in a great relationship.
I'd pick a lively dinner party over a quiet night in almost every time.
It's important to me that my partner and I have an active social life together.
I have a pretty clear picture of where I want to be in five years.
I tend to figure things out as I go rather than mapping out a long-term plan.
I want a partner who's as ambitious about their career as I am about mine.
Financial goals and milestones (savings targets, investment plans) are something I actively track.
The best things in my life have come from spontaneous decisions, not careful planning.

scoring.sub_scale_computation: Each sub-scale score = mean of its 5 items (after reversals). Range: 1.0 - 5.0. Higher scores indicate faster pace / more social / more future-oriented.
scoring.dimension_score_computation: Dimension score = mean of all 15 items (after reversals). Range: 1.0 - 5.0.
scoring.compatibility_function: similarity: pair_score = 1 - (|score_A - score_B| / 4). Sub-scale similarity is also computed independently: sub_scale_pair_score = 1 - (|sub_A - sub_B| / 4). Final compatibility = 0.4 * dimension_similarity + 0.6 * mean(sub_scale_similarities). The sub-scale weighting matters here because a couple can have the same overall score but mismatch on critical sub-scales (e.g., both score 3.0 overall, but one is high-pace/low-social and the other is low-pace/high-social).

dimensions.title: Love Languages
dimensions.description: Identifies how you naturally give and receive love, and how flexible you are in adapting to a partner's preferences. The goal is not to match -- it's to ensure what you give is what they need.
dimensions.scientific_basis: Based on Chapman's Five Love Languages framework (Chapman, 1992), operationalized through a forced-choice pair methodology similar to Egbert & Polk's (2006) Love Languages Scale. The forced-choice format reduces social desirability bias and produces a rank ordering rather than inflated endorsement across all languages. Language Flexibility items draw from Lemay, Clark, & Feeney's (2007) work on responsiveness in relationships.
dimensions.compatibility_type_rationale: Love languages are about the give-receive loop. What matters is not whether two people share the same love language, but whether Partner A's natural giving language matches Partner B's primary receiving language, and vice versa. A person who naturally gives acts of service paired with a person who most values receiving acts of service is a strong match -- even if their own receiving preferences differ entirely. Language Flexibility moderates the score: high flexibility in one or both partners compensates for give-receive mismatches.

sub_scales.title: Primary Receiving Language
sub_scales.description: How you most prefer to receive love. Determined by forced-choice pairs.

sub_scales.title: Primary Giving Language
sub_scales.description: How you naturally express love to your partner. Determined by forced-choice pairs.

sub_scales.title: Language Flexibility
sub_scales.description: How willing and able you are to express love in ways that don't come naturally to you.

questions.text: Which feels more meaningful to you?

a.text: Your partner tells you specifically what they love about you

b.text: Your partner plans a full day for just the two of you, no phones

questions.text: Which would make you feel more loved?

a.text: Your partner surprises you with a thoughtful gift that shows they really know you

b.text: Your partner holds your hand, rubs your back, or pulls you close without being asked

questions.text: Which hits harder after a rough week?

a.text: Your partner handles dinner, dishes, and your errands so you can rest

b.text: Your partner writes you a note saying how proud they are of you

questions.text: Which makes you feel more connected?

a.text: Sitting together on the couch, legs intertwined, not necessarily talking

b.text: Your partner takes something off your plate without you having to ask

questions.text: Which means more on your birthday?

a.text: Your partner clears their whole schedule to spend the day with you

b.text: Your partner finds you a gift you mentioned wanting months ago

questions.text: When your partner is stressed, what's your first instinct?

a.text: Tell them you believe in them and remind them what they're great at

b.text: Take over some of their responsibilities so they have less on their plate

questions.text: How do you most naturally show someone you're thinking of them?

a.text: Pick up something small you know they'd love

b.text: Send a text telling them what you appreciate about them

questions.text: On a random Tuesday night, how do you show love?

a.text: Put your phone away and give them your full, undivided attention

b.text: Reach over and give them a long hug or a shoulder massage

questions.text: When you want to celebrate your partner's win, what do you do?

a.text: Take them somewhere special or plan a night out together

b.text: Buy or make them something to mark the occasion

questions.text: When your partner seems down, what comes most naturally?

a.text: Pull them close, hold them, be physically present

b.text: Handle whatever's stressing them out -- cook, clean, fix something

questions.text: If I found out my partner feels most loved through something that doesn't come naturally to me, I'd put in the effort to learn.
I think people shouldn't have to change how they express love -- your partner should appreciate your natural style.
I've successfully adapted how I show love in past relationships based on what my partner needed.
Even if it feels awkward at first, I'm willing to express love in new ways if it matters to my partner.
Love should feel effortless -- if you have to think about how to show it, something's off.

forced_choice_scoring.description: For questions ll_01 through ll_10, tally the number of times each love language is selected. Each language can appear 0-4 times in receiving (ll_01-ll_05) and 0-4 times in giving (ll_06-ll_10). Each language appears in exactly 2 of the 5 receiving questions and 2 of the 5 giving questions.
forced_choice_scoring.receiving_language_profile: Count selections per language across ll_01-ll_05. Primary receiving language = language with highest count. If tied, both are recorded as co-primary.
forced_choice_scoring.giving_language_profile: Count selections per language across ll_06-ll_10. Primary giving language = language with highest count. If tied, both are recorded as co-primary.

likert_scoring.flexibility_score: Mean of ll_11 through ll_15 (after reversals). Range: 1.0 - 5.0.

scoring.compatibility_function: complementarity: (1) Compute give-receive match score: check if Partner A's primary giving language matches Partner B's primary receiving language, and vice versa. Full match (both directions) = 1.0, one-direction match = 0.6, no match = 0.2. If co-primary languages exist, partial credit is given for any overlap. (2) Compute flexibility modifier: flexibility_mod = mean(flex_A, flex_B) normalized to 0.0-1.0 range. (3) Final pair_score = base_match * (0.6 + 0.4 * flexibility_mod). High flexibility rescues mismatched languages (up to 0.4 bonus); low flexibility makes even matched languages slightly less effective. (4) A perfectly matched pair with high flexibility scores ~1.0; a mismatched pair with low flexibility scores ~0.12.

metadata.notes: These dimensions (4-6) complement dimensions 1-3 to form the complete 6-dimension CompatibleIQ assessment. Combined weight of all 6 dimensions should equal 100. Dimensions 1-3 carry the remaining 58 points.
